stem: The nurse is caring for a 62-year-old client who developed acute chest pain and dyspnea. Based on the provided electronic health record, complete the bowtie item. Select the potential condition the client is experiencing, two actions to take, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
Situation: ED nurse reporting on a 62-year-old male, John Doe. At 07:00, he developed acute pleuritic chest pain and severe dyspnea.
Background: He has a 40-pack-year smoking history and returned from a 14-hour flight yesterday. He is not on anticoagulants.
Assessment: Vitals: BP 92/58, HR 128, RR 32, SpO2 84% on room air. EKG shows S1Q3T3 pattern. D-dimer is 4,200 ng/mL; Troponin I is 0.12 ng/mL.
Recommendation: I suspect a massive pulmonary embolism. I recommend initiating high-flow oxygen and preparing for an emergent CT pulmonary angiogram.

tabs.title: Laboratory Results
tabs.content:
TestResultReference Range
Troponin I0.12 ng/mL<0.04 ng/mL
BNP800 pg/mL<100 pg/mL
D-Dimer4,200 ng/mL FEU<500 ng/mL FEU
ABG7.32/55/60/24/-2

tabs.title: Diagnostics
tabs.content: 12-Lead EKG (07:05): Sinus tachycardia at 128/min. S1Q3T3 pattern noted. T-wave inversions in leads V1-V4. Consistent with right ventricular strain.

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
General: Anxious, diaphoretic, labored breathing.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardia, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Respiratory: Increased work of breathing, diminished breath sounds on the right side, coarse crackles bilaterally.
Neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time.
Integumentary: Pale, cool, and clammy skin.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content:
CT Pulmonary Angiogram Report (Preliminary):
Large saddle pulmonary embolus noted at the bifurcation of the main pulmonary artery, with extension into the right and left pulmonary arteries. Evidence of right ventricular enlargement and bowing of the interventricular septum toward the left ventricle, suggestive of acute right heart strain. No other acute findings.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content:
Nursing Priorities:
  1. Optimize oxygenation and ventilation.
  2. Prevent further clot propagation.
  3. Monitor for complications of PE and anticoagulation.
  4. Provide emotional support and education.
Nursing Interventions:
  1. Administer high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask.
  2. Initiate continuous cardiac monitoring.
  3. Insert a large-bore IV for fluid and medication administration.
  4. Elevate the head of the bed to 45 degrees.
  5. Administer prescribed anticoagulants (e.g., heparin).
  6. Monitor vital signs, SpO2, and EKG frequently.
  7. Assess for bleeding and signs of hemodynamic instability.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content:
MedicationDosageRouteFrequencyPRN
Heparin Sodium80 units/kg bolus, then 18 units/kg/hrIVContinuousNo
Oxygen10-15 L/minNon-rebreather MaskContinuousYes, for SpO2 < 90%
Normal Saline250 mL bolusIVOnceYes, for hypotension (SBP < 90 mmHg)

conditionOptions.text: Massive Pulmonary Embolism (PE) with Right Heart Strain

actionOptions.text: Elevate the head of the bed and provide high-flow oxygen
Initiate IV Heparin and prepare for CT pulmonary angiogram
Encourage the client to cough and deep breathe
Place the client in a left-lateral Trendelenburg position

parameterOptions.text: EKG for signs of right ventricular strain
Presence of a positive Babinski sign

conditions.c1: While troponin is elevated, the pleuritic pain, S1Q3T3 EKG pattern, and critically high D-dimer are more specific for a massive PE. The hypotension is due to obstructive shock, not cardiogenic shock from MI.
conditions.c2: The client's recent long flight, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, hypotension, S1Q3T3 EKG pattern, and markedly elevated D-dimer are classic signs of a massive PE causing obstructive shock and right ventricular strain.
conditions.c3: Pericarditis typically presents with positional chest pain relieved by leaning forward and diffuse ST elevation on EKG, which are absent here. The client's presentation indicates a more acute, life-threatening vascular obstruction.

actions.a1: Elevating the head of the bed improves lung expansion, and high-flow oxygen is critical to correct severe hypoxemia (SpO2 84%) caused by the ventilation/perfusion mismatch from the pulmonary embolism.
actions.a2: Initiating IV heparin is a priority to prevent further clot formation. A CT pulmonary angiogram is the gold standard for definitive diagnosis and guides further treatment like thrombolysis.
actions.a3: While important for atelectasis prevention, vigorous coughing can worsen pleuritic chest pain and increase myocardial oxygen demand in a hemodynamically unstable client. It is not a priority intervention.
actions.a4: This position is used for air embolism. In a massive PE, increasing preload to an already strained right ventricle by placing the client in Trendelenburg can worsen hemodynamic collapse.

parameters.p1: Monitoring for EKG changes indicative of right ventricular strain (e.g., S1Q3T3, new RBBB) is crucial for assessing the hemodynamic impact of the PE and the effectiveness of treatment.
parameters.p2: Oxygen saturation and respiratory effort are direct indicators of gas exchange and the severity of the ventilation/perfusion mismatch. Worsening hypoxia or increased work of breathing signals clinical deterioration.
parameters.p3: While important for general metabolic status, fasting serum glucose is not a priority parameter for monitoring the immediate life-threatening consequences of a massive pulmonary embolism.
parameters.p4: A Babinski sign assesses for upper motor neuron lesions and is irrelevant to the pathophysiology of a pulmonary embolism. The priority is monitoring cardiopulmonary status.

pearls.title: Early Recognition of PE
pearls.description: Prompt recognition of PE is crucial. Look for risk factors (recent surgery, immobility, hypercoagulable state), signs (sudden dyspnea, chest pain), and symptoms (tachycardia, tachypnea, hypoxia).
pearls.strategy: Maintain a high index of suspicion in at-risk patients. Use clinical prediction rules (e.g., Wells score) to assess pretest probability.

pearls.title: Importance of Anticoagulation
pearls.description: Anticoagulation is the cornerstone of PE treatment. It prevents further clot propagation and allows the body's natural thrombolytic mechanisms to dissolve the existing clot.
pearls.strategy: Initiate anticoagulation as soon as PE is suspected, unless contraindicated. Use appropriate agents (e.g., heparin, enoxaparin, fondaparinux, direct oral anticoagulants) based on patient-specific factors.

traps.title: Misinterpreting EKG Findings
traps.description: The classic S1Q3T3 pattern is neither sensitive nor specific for PE. Relying solely on this finding can lead to missed or delayed diagnosis.
traps.strategy: Interpret EKG findings in conjunction with other clinical and diagnostic data. Consider alternative diagnoses if the EKG is not consistent with PE.

traps.title: Overlooking Right Heart Strain
traps.description: Right heart strain can lead to hemodynamic instability and poor outcomes. Failure to recognize and address right heart strain can result in cardiogenic shock and death.
traps.strategy: Assess for signs of right heart strain (e.g., elevated JVP, right ventricular enlargement on echocardiography). Consider advanced therapies (e.g., thrombolysis, embolectomy) in patients with severe right heart strain.

mnemonics.title: PERC Rule
mnemonics.description: A mnemonic to remember the Pulmonary Embolism Rule-out Criteria (PERC): SaO2 ≥95%, HR <100 bpm, RR <20 breaths/min, no hemoptysis, no estrogen use, no prior DVT/PE, no unilateral leg swelling, age <50 years. If all criteria are met, PE is unlikely.

mnemonics.title: HAT
mnemonics.description: HAT: Heparin, Antibodies, Thrombocytopenia. Remember to monitor platelets when administering heparin.

answerBreakdown.c1: While troponin is elevated, the pleuritic pain, S1Q3T3 EKG pattern, and critically high D-dimer are more specific for a massive PE. The hypotension is due to obstructive shock, not cardiogenic shock from MI.
answerBreakdown.c2: The client's recent long flight, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, hypotension, S1Q3T3 EKG pattern, and markedly elevated D-dimer are classic signs of a massive PE causing obstructive shock and right ventricular strain.
answerBreakdown.c3: Pericarditis typically presents with positional chest pain relieved by leaning forward and diffuse ST elevation on EKG, which are absent here. The client's presentation indicates a more acute, life-threatening vascular obstruction.
answerBreakdown.a1: Elevating the head of the bed improves lung expansion, and high-flow oxygen is critical to correct severe hypoxemia (SpO2 84%) caused by the ventilation/perfusion mismatch from the pulmonary embolism.
answerBreakdown.a2: Initiating IV heparin is a priority to prevent further clot formation. A CT pulmonary angiogram is the gold standard for definitive diagnosis and guides further treatment like thrombolysis.
answerBreakdown.a3: While important for atelectasis prevention, vigorous coughing can worsen pleuritic chest pain and increase myocardial oxygen demand in a hemodynamically unstable client. It is not a priority intervention.
answerBreakdown.a4: This position is used for air embolism. In a massive PE, increasing preload to an already strained right ventricle by placing the client in Trendelenburg can worsen hemodynamic collapse.
answerBreakdown.p1: Monitoring for EKG changes indicative of right ventricular strain (e.g., S1Q3T3, new RBBB) is crucial for assessing the hemodynamic impact of the PE and the effectiveness of treatment.
answerBreakdown.p2: Oxygen saturation and respiratory effort are direct indicators of gas exchange and the severity of the ventilation/perfusion mismatch. Worsening hypoxia or increased work of breathing signals clinical deterioration.
answerBreakdown.p3: While important for general metabolic status, fasting serum glucose is not a priority parameter for monitoring the immediate life-threatening consequences of a massive pulmonary embolism.
answerBreakdown.p4: A Babinski sign assesses for upper motor neuron lesions and is irrelevant to the pathophysiology of a pulmonary embolism. The priority is monitoring cardiopulmonary status.
